lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Welcome, sweet prince, to London, to your chamber.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Welcome, dear cousin, my thoughts’ sovereign:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The weary way hath made you melancholy.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: No, uncle; but our crosses on the way

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Have made it tedious, wearisome, and heavy:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: I want more uncles here to welcome me.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Sweet prince, the untainted virtue of your years

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Hath not yet dived into the world’s deceit:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Nor more can you distinguish of a man

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Than of his outward show; which, God he knows,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Seldom or never jumpeth with the heart.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Those uncles which you want were dangerous;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Your grace attended to their sugar’d words,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But look’d not on the poison of their hearts:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: God keep you from them, and from such false friends!

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: God keep me from false friends! but they were none.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My lord, the mayor of London comes to greet you.

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: God bless your grace with health and happy days!

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: I thank you, good my lord; and thank you all.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: I thought my mother, and my brother York,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Would long ere this have met us on the way:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Fie, what a slug is Hastings, that he comes not

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: To tell us whether they will come or no!

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And, in good time, here comes the sweating lord.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Welcome, my lord: what, will our mother come?

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: On what occasion, God he knows, not I,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: The queen your mother, and your brother York,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Have taken sanctuary: the tender prince

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Would fain have come with me to meet your grace,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: But by his mother was perforce withheld.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Fie, what an indirect and peevish course

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Is this of hers! Lord cardinal, will your grace

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Persuade the queen to send the Duke of York

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Unto his princely brother presently?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: If she deny, Lord Hastings, go with him,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And from her jealous arms pluck him perforce.

lines.speaker: CARDINAL
lines.text: My Lord of Buckingham, if my weak oratory

lines.speaker: CARDINAL
lines.text: Can from his mother win the Duke of York,

lines.speaker: CARDINAL
lines.text: Anon expect him here; but if she be obdurate

lines.speaker: CARDINAL
lines.text: To mild entreaties, God in heaven forbid

lines.speaker: CARDINAL
lines.text: We should infringe the holy privilege

lines.speaker: CARDINAL
lines.text: Of blessed sanctuary! not for all this land

lines.speaker: CARDINAL
lines.text: Would I be guilty of so deep a sin.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: You are too senseless-obstinate, my lord,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Too ceremonious and traditional:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Weigh it but with the grossness of this age,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: You break not sanctuary in seizing him.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: The benefit thereof is always granted

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To those whose dealings have deserved the place,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And those who have the wit to claim the place:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: This prince hath neither claim’d it nor deserved it;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And therefore, in mine opinion, cannot have it:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Then, taking him from thence that is not there,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: You break no privilege nor charter there.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Oft have I heard of sanctuary men;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: But sanctuary children ne’er till now.

lines.speaker: CARDINAL
lines.text: My lord, you shall o’er-rule my mind for once.

lines.speaker: CARDINAL
lines.text: Come on, Lord Hastings, will you go with me?

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I go, my lord.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Good lords, make all the speedy haste you may.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Say, uncle Gloucester, if our brother come,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Where shall we sojourn till our coronation?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Where it seems best unto your royal self.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: If I may counsel you, some day or two

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Your highness shall repose you at the Tower:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Then where you please, and shall be thought most fit

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: For your best health and recreation.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: I do not like the Tower, of any place.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Did Julius Caesar build that place, my lord?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: He did, my gracious lord, begin that place;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Which, since, succeeding ages have re-edified.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Is it upon record, or else reported

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Successively from age to age, he built it?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Upon record, my gracious lord.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: But say, my lord, it were not register’d,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Methinks the truth should live from age to age,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: As ’twere retail’d to all posterity,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Even to the general all-ending day.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: So wise so young, they say, do never live long.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: What say you, uncle?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I say, without characters, fame lives long.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Thus, like the formal vice, Iniquity,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I moralize two meanings in one word.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: That Julius Caesar was a famous man;

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: With what his valour did enrich his wit,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: His wit set down to make his valour live:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Death makes no conquest of this conqueror;

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: For now he lives in fame, though not in life.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: I’ll tell you what, my cousin Buckingham—

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: What, my gracious lord?

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: An if I live until I be a man,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: I’ll win our ancient right in France again,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Or die a soldier, as I lived a king.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Short summers lightly have a forward spring.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Now, in good time, here comes the Duke of York.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Richard of York! how fares our loving brother?

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: Well, my dread lord; so must I call you now.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Ay, brother, to our grief, as it is yours:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Too late he died that might have kept that title,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Which by his death hath lost much majesty.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: How fares our cousin, noble Lord of York?

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: I thank you, gentle uncle. O, my lord,

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: You said that idle weeds are fast in growth:

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: The prince my brother hath outgrown me far.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He hath, my lord.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: And therefore is he idle?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: O, my fair cousin, I must not say so.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: Then is he more beholding to you than I.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He may command me as my sovereign;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But you have power in me as in a kinsman.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: I pray you, uncle, give me this dagger.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My dagger, little cousin? with all my heart.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: A beggar, brother?

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: Of my kind uncle, that I know will give;

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: And being but a toy, which is no grief to give.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: A greater gift than that I’ll give my cousin.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: A greater gift! O, that’s the sword to it.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Ay, gentle cousin, were it light enough.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: O, then, I see, you will part but with light gifts;

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: In weightier things you’ll say a beggar nay.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: It is too heavy for your grace to wear.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: I weigh it lightly, were it heavier.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What, would you have my weapon, little lord?

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: I would, that I might thank you as you call me.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: How?

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: Little.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: My Lord of York will still be cross in talk:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Uncle, your grace knows how to bear with him.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: You mean, to bear me, not to bear with me:

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: Uncle, my brother mocks both you and me;

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: Because that I am little, like an ape,

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: He thinks that you should bear me on your shoulders.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: With what a sharp-provided wit he reasons!

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To mitigate the scorn he gives his uncle,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: He prettily and aptly taunts himself:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: So cunning and so young is wonderful.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My lord, will’t please you pass along?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Myself and my good cousin Buckingham

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Will to your mother, to entreat of her

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To meet you at the Tower and welcome you.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: What, will you go unto the Tower, my lord?

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: My lord protector needs will have it so.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: I shall not sleep in quiet at the Tower.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Why, what should you fear?

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: Marry, my uncle Clarence’ angry ghost:

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: My grandam told me he was murder’d there.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: I fear no uncles dead.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Nor none that live, I hope.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: An if they live, I hope I need not fear.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: But come, my lord; and with a heavy heart,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Thinking on them, go I unto the Tower. A Sennet. Exeunt all but Gloucester, Buckingham and Catesby.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Think you, my lord, this little prating York

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Was not incensed by his subtle mother

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To taunt and scorn you thus opprobriously?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: No doubt, no doubt: O, ’tis a parlous boy;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Bold, quick, ingenious, forward, capable:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He is all the mother’s, from the top to toe.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Well, let them rest. Come hither, Catesby.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Thou art sworn as deeply to effect what we intend

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: As closely to conceal what we impart:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Thou know’st our reasons urged upon the way;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: What think’st thou? is it not an easy matter

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To make William Lord Hastings of our mind,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: For the instalment of this noble duke

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: In the seat royal of this famous isle?

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: He for his father’s sake so loves the prince,

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: That he will not be won to aught against him.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: What think’st thou, then, of Stanley? what will he?

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: He will do all in all as Hastings doth.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Well, then, no more but this: go, gentle Catesby,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And, as it were far off, sound thou Lord Hastings,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: How doth he stand affected to our purpose;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And summon him to-morrow to the Tower,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To sit about the coronation.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: If thou dost find him tractable to us,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Encourage him, and show him all our reasons:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: If he be leaden, icy-cold, unwilling,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Be thou so too; and so break off your talk,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And give us notice of his inclination:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: For we to-morrow hold divided councils,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Wherein thyself shalt highly be employ’d.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Commend me to Lord William: tell him, Catesby,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: His ancient knot of dangerous adversaries

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To-morrow are let blood at Pomfret-castle;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And bid my friend, for joy of this good news,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Give Mistress Shore one gentle kiss the more.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Good Catesby, go, effect this business soundly.

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: My good lords both, with all the heed I may.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Shall we hear from you, Catesby, ere we sleep?

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: You shall, my lord.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: At Crosby Place, there shall you find us both.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Now, my lord, what shall we do, if we perceive

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Lord Hastings will not yield to our complots?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Chop off his head, man; somewhat we will do:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And, look, when I am king, claim thou of me

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The earldom of Hereford, and the moveables

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Whereof the king my brother stood possess’d.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: I’ll claim that promise at your grace’s hands.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And look to have it yielded with all willingness.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Come, let us sup betimes, that afterwards

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: We may digest our complots in some form.

stage_directions.text: The trumpets sound. Enter the young Prince, the Dukes ofGloucester and Buckingham, Cardinal Bourchier, Catesby, and others.
Enter the Lord Mayor, and his train.
Enter young York, Hastings, and the Cardinal.

glosses.definition: London, ceremonially called 'the king's chamber' — Camera Regis, a title of honour
hardships, troubles (literal crosses borne on the way)
matches, corresponds to the heart
sluggish fellow; lazy person
perverse and willful
seize him by force
hardened, unyielding

glosses.phrase: infringe the holy privilege / Of blessed sanctuary
glosses.definition: violate the sacred right of sanctuary — church ground that barred civil arrest
stubbornly stupid
Buckingham's novelty — a coinage for his argument that children can't claim sanctuary; theologically dubious
the White Tower — tradition attributed its foundations to Caesar; actually built by William the Conqueror
rebuilt; re-built upon
the Day of Judgement; doomsday

glosses.phrase: So wise so young, they say, do never live long
glosses.definition: proverbial; Gloucester's aside, telegraphing the boy's fate
without written letters — fame lives in memory, not just script
the Vice-figure 'Iniquity' of the old morality plays — moral abstraction turned stage character. Gloucester names his dramatic ancestry

glosses.phrase: moralize two meanings in one word
glosses.definition: make one word bear double meaning — the Vice's signature equivocation

glosses.phrase: Short summers lightly have a forward spring
glosses.definition: brief summers are apt to have an early spring — proverbial; precocity portends early death
lord to be feared; standard address to a king

glosses.phrase: idle weeds are fast in growth
glosses.definition: York throws Gloucester's own proverb back at him — cf. 2.4.13
York's punning insult about being carried on shoulders — an ape on a fool's back was a common fool-figure
readily-furnished, quick intelligence
the boy anticipates his uncle Clarence's vengeful spirit — more telegraph of coming Tower deaths
stage direction — a ceremonial fanfare of trumpets
chattering, talkative
with reproach; scornfully
dangerously precocious
two separate meetings held at once — Richard's tactic to sift Hastings' loyalty
long-standing group of enemies — Rivers, Grey, Vaughan
bled (killed) at Pomfret — surgical/medical euphemism for execution
conspiracies, secret plots
Gloucester's notorious casual sentence on Hastings — the line's brutality is the point
Buckingham's promised reward — historically claimed by Buckingham through a maternal line
work out, arrange our plots — 'digest' = organize (from rhetoric)

annotations.title: "Sanctuary children" — Buckingham's innovation
annotations.body: Buckingham's argument that Prince York can be taken from Westminster sanctuary because "sanctuary children" are a novel and unsupportable category is dramaturgically audacious and historically consequential. The right of sanctuary at Westminster Abbey, established by Edward the Confessor's charter and confirmed by successive kings, was a live and contested legal question in 1483. The Cardinal's hesitation ("God in heaven forbid / We should infringe the holy privilege / Of blessed sanctuary!") represents the orthodox position; Buckingham's counter that only those with "wit to claim" the place can hold it overturns centuries of precedent. Historically the boy was indeed removed from sanctuary under these arguments, a decision that led directly to the princes' deaths in the Tower. Shakespeare compresses the theological and legal dispute into eight lines and lets Buckingham win — because the argument's success on the stage is the fact that produces the murder.
sources: Thomas More, History of King Richard III (c. 1513)
A. R. Myers, England in the Late Middle Ages (1952)

annotations.title: "So wise so young, they say, do never live long"
annotations.body: Richard's aside as the young prince reflects on history — "So wise so young, they say, do never live long" — is the play's most chilling aphoristic sentence of prediction. The young Edward V has just delivered a compressed meditation on historical memory: Julius Caesar's wit set down his valour to make it "live," so death "makes no conquest of this conqueror." The speech is a twelve-year-old's theory of posterity, and it is well done. Richard's response is to note that children who speak like this are marked. The aside is then doubled a moment later when Gloucester tells the audience directly — in a further aside — that he speaks "like the formal vice, Iniquity / I moralize two meanings in one word." The self-naming as Vice-figure is explicit, and it is paired with a line that functions as the boy's death-sentence.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)
Bernard Spivack, Shakespeare and the Allegory of Evil (1958)

annotations.title: York's wit and the play's rehearsal of the princes' intelligence
annotations.body: The exchange between Richard and the Duke of York is a deliberate showcase: Shakespeare makes the younger prince, like the elder, visibly clever. York teases Richard about his dagger, about small herbs and great weeds (turning Richard's own 2.4 proverb back on him), and about being carried like an ape on shoulders. Buckingham's aside — "So cunning and so young is wonderful" — is the scene's most quietly devastating line. In a court where political survival depends on obscurity, these boys have shown themselves brilliantly in conversation. The scene thereby completes the indictment implicit in 2.4: the princes are too conspicuous to survive. Richard's plan, announced to Buckingham and Catesby in the remainder of the scene, is the logical consequence of a political order in which a clever child is a threat.
sources: Ann Blake, "Children and Suffering in Shakespeare's Plays" (1998)
Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)

annotations.title: "Chop off his head" — the casualness of the new regime
annotations.body: Richard's instruction to Buckingham about Hastings — "Chop off his head, man; somewhat we will do" — is the play's bluntest sentence of political killing, and its placement is deliberate. We have just watched Richard charm and condescend to two boys in measured iambs; he pivots to casual dismembership in a single line. The tonal whiplash is the characterization. Richard governs by emotional range: tenderness, legalism, piety, mock-humility, and dismissal each available at will. The command's casualness — "somewhat we will do" — is what marks the new regime. Kingship under Edward IV had required ceremony; Richard has stripped ceremony from the act of killing while preserving it for public display. This is the play's theory of tyranny in miniature.
sources: A. P. Rossiter, Angel with Horns (1961)
Stephen Greenblatt, Tyrant: Shakespeare on Politics (2018)

trials.prompt: Buckingham's argument that the young Duke of York can be removed from Westminster sanctuary because he is a child turns on what claim?
options: That no sanctuary has ever existed in English law
That sanctuary protects only adults with "the wit to claim" it, making "sanctuary children" a novel category without legal precedent
That Westminster Abbey has lost its sanctuary privilege under a recent statute
That the boy is not in fact in sanctuary because he has already left the grounds

trials.prompt: Richard's aside "So wise so young, they say, do never live long" functions as what?
options: An idle proverbial observation with no plot consequence
A death-sentence delivered in the form of aphorism — Richard predicting (and effectively confirming) the boy's fate
A genuine expression of concern for his nephew's health
A prayer that the prince be long-lived

trials.prompt: Which figure does Gloucester explicitly compare himself to in his aside?
options: The Roman general Coriolanus
The "formal Vice, Iniquity" of the old morality plays, who "moralizes two meanings in one word"
The biblical Cain
The conqueror Tamerlane

trials.prompt: The princes' visible cleverness in this scene serves what dramatic function?
options: It establishes them as comic relief before the tragic turn
It completes the indictment begun in 2.4: the boys are too conspicuous to survive in Richard's political order
It suggests they will outwit Richard in a later scene
It demonstrates the high quality of Tudor royal education

trials.prompt: Richard's line "Chop off his head, man; somewhat we will do" about Hastings achieves what tonal effect?
options: It is a quotation from a legal statute
Tonal whiplash: the measured iambs of the prior exchange pivot to casual dismembership, revealing a regime in which killing requires no ceremony
A misheard line that the audience would have mistaken for courtly politeness
An attempt at humour to defuse tension
